title: The Night Before
tagline: Full moon tomorrow. Everyone is ready. Almost. Achilles's heel is aching again — the worst it's ever been.
arc_note: PRE-FINALE QUIET EPISODE — The night before the Titan arrives. Each character has a private moment. The comedy is softer here, the warmth is higher. The Achilles heel arc reaches its most serious point: he tells Zeus for the first time that his heel hurts, and Zeus actually listens. Medusa is confirmed coming. Poseidon confirms ready. All pieces in place. Final tone: we've been preparing for this whole season without knowing it.

scenes.title: Sunset Before
scenes.narration: The night before the full moon, Olympus was quiet. Athena had finished the final plan. The roles were assigned. The positions were mapped. Everyone had been told what to do. For one evening, there was nothing left to prepare. Archie sat on the balcony railing watching the sun go down. He looked at the camera. He looked at the Olympus Sea. The dolphins were visible from here. Poseidon had been right — it was actually quite beautiful.
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. QUIET OPENING — Olympus at sunset. The plan is complete. Athena's scroll rolled up and shelved. Archie alone on the balcony railing, watching the sun go down over the Olympus Sea. Dolphins visible in the water below. Warm golden light. He looks at the viewer. He looks at the sea. This is beautiful. He knew it would be. 16:9.

scenes.title: Hercules Rereads His Scrolls
scenes.narration: Hercules had read the three engineering scrolls six times. He was reading them again. Right-way up. He had marked the sections relevant to tomorrow with small tabs he'd made from his spare lion cape fabric — the same fabric as his scroll case. He had notes in the margins. Small, careful, surprisingly neat notes. He looked up when Athena walked by. He said: 'I know which pressure points I'm responsible for.' She said: 'I know you do.' He nodded and went back to the scroll.
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. Hercules alone with his three scrolls by lamplight — reading right-way up, six times through, small careful tabs marking relevant sections, neat margin notes. Athena walks past, they exchange a quiet confirmation. He nods and returns to his reading. This is the most serious we've ever seen Hercules. He is ready. 16:9.

scenes.title: Medusa's Reply
scenes.narration: Perseus had sent Medusa a message that morning. The reply had arrived an hour ago. He'd been reading it on the Olympus Sea steps, alone. It said: 'I will be at the east valley at dawn. I know the cracks you mean — I've seen them from the garden edge. I will need Perseus to tell me exactly where to stand. I do not want to turn anything I am not supposed to.' He folded it carefully. He'd sent back: 'I'll be there.' She would come. She had said so clearly.
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. Perseus alone on the Olympus Sea steps at dusk, reading Medusa's reply carefully. Her message folded carefully, his reply already sent. The sea behind him. He looks at the water with quiet relief and something more complicated — she came through. She said clearly she was coming. That matters to him more than he expected. 16:9.

scenes.title: He Tells Zeus
scenes.narration: Achilles found Zeus on the practice field after dark, still running his position sequence. His heel had been aching since Tuesday. Tonight it was the worst it had ever been. He had been saying he was fine. He found Zeus and said: 'My heel is bad tonight.' Not 'I'm fine.' Just: it's bad. Zeus stopped his sequence. He didn't ask if Achilles was sure. He didn't suggest maybe it was nothing. He said: 'How bad?' Achilles said: 'Bad enough to tell you.' Zeus said: 'Okay. Tell me what you need tomorrow.'
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. ACHILLES AND ZEUS — night, practice field. Achilles approaching Zeus who is running his sequence. The honest exchange — first time Achilles has dropped the running joke entirely. Zeus stopping and listening without dismissing. 'How bad?' 'Bad enough to tell you.' The genuine care between them. This is what their friendship looks like when the jokes are set down. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Position Change
scenes.narration: Zeus came back with Athena's plan. He found the crack locations. He found the one closest to the main path — the one Achilles was supposed to run coordination from. He went to Athena. He said: 'The coordination position is at the top of the east stairs. Achilles would have to run to reach anyone who needs repositioning. Can the position move?' Athena looked at the plan. She looked at Zeus. She said: 'Yes. The coordination position can move ten meters left. Nothing changes.' She moved the mark. She didn't ask why.
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. Zeus with the plan, finding the coordination position, going to Athena with a specific question. She looks at the plan. She moves the mark. She doesn't ask why — she can see why. Athena and Zeus: two people who care about the same people and know how to protect each other's dignity. The small adjustment that means Achilles won't have to run. 16:9.

scenes.title: Uncle Poseidon Writes Back
scenes.narration: Poseidon's reply arrived with a live sardine somehow wrapped around it. The letter said: 'I understand. I will position the sea-draw at the west angle as your girl with glasses described. The dolphins have been briefed. They are very motivated. I may also bring one additional surprise that I think you will like. Love, your favorite uncle. P.S. I know I am your only uncle on this side but I believe I would still be the favorite.' At the bottom: a small dolphin drawing. Zeus smiled.
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. Zeus reading Poseidon's reply — a scroll with a live sardine wrapped around it. The letter with its characteristically Poseidon voice — enthusiastic, slightly competitive about 'favorite uncle.' The dolphin drawing at the bottom. Zeus smiling, alone in the lamplight. The Olympus Sea visible outside. His uncle is on their side. 16:9.

scenes.title: Before the Finale
scenes.narration: Before the biggest thing any of them had ever done, they each took one evening. Hercules read his scrolls. Perseus read his letter. Achilles told the truth about his heel. Zeus moved a mark on a plan. Poseidon sent a sardine. Athena watched from the balcony with Archie. Archie looked at the camera. He was not concerned. He had been watching these people for three years. They were ready. Tomorrow: the season finale.
scenes.visual_prompt: Little Olympus kids cartoon style. FINAL PRE-FINALE CARD — the group together on the balcony at night, looking at the full moon rising over the Olympus Sea. Each of them visible: Hercules with his scrolls, Perseus with the folded letter, Achilles standing beside Zeus, Athena with the master scroll. Archie looks at the camera. He is completely calm. He has seen three years of these people. He knows they'll be okay. Full moon. Tomorrow is the day. 16:9.

lesson: Before the biggest challenges, the most important thing isn't more preparation — it's telling the truth to the people next to you, and trusting them with it.
achilles_arc_note: HEEL ARC PEAK: He says it plainly for the first time. Zeus responds without minimizing. Athena adjusts the plan without asking why. This is the arc resolution — not that the heel is fixed, but that he's not alone with it anymore.
youtube_title: The Night Before Everything Changes... 🌕 | Little Olympus Episode 19
next_episode_preview: Episode 20 — THE SEASON FINALE. The full moon rises. The Titan stirs. Everyone takes their position. And someone unexpected shows up when it matters most.
